sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.content: Cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma (CSCC) generally has a good prognosis, with a 5-year survival rate of approximately 98%. However, treatment continues to evolve to improve quality of life and minimize recurrence. Metastatic CSCC is rare, occurring in an estimated 1.9% to 2.7% of all cases, with nodal metastases and distant metastatic disease estimated at 3.7% and 0.4%, respectively. One-third of deaths from CSCC occur from local disease alone.

factors.factor: Anatomic location (head, neck, hands, feet, pretibia, anogenital area are high-risk)
factors.detail: High-risk anatomic locations include head, neck, hands, feet, pretibia, and anogenital area.

factors.factor: Tumor diameter (≥2 cm and especially ≥4 cm)
factors.detail: 3-year disease-specific survival rates are 93% for lesions <4 cm and 67% for lesions ≥4 cm (P = .0003).
Poorly defined clinical borders are a high-risk feature.
Recurrent disease carries a higher risk.
5-year survival is 52% for immunosuppressed patients (e.g., transplant recipients or those with hematologic malignancies) compared to 72% for immunocompetent patients (AJCC 7th edition).

factors.factor: Site of prior radiation therapy or chronic inflammation
factors.detail: Prior radiation or chronic inflammation increases risk.
Rapid growth is a high-risk feature.
Clinical neurologic symptoms indicate perineural invasion and poorer outcomes.
Poorly differentiated tumors have higher risk of recurrence and metastasis.
Adenosquamous and sarcomatoid subtypes are high-risk.

factors.factor: Depth of invasion (>6 mm or invasion beyond subcutaneous fat)
factors.detail: Invasion into subcutaneous fat significantly increases rates of recurrence and metastasis.

factors.factor: Perineural involvement (tumor cells within nerve sheath of a nerve deep to dermis or ≥0.1 mm caliber)
factors.detail: Perineural invasion is associated with increased risks of recurrence, metastasis, and death. Poorer outcomes are associated with clinical symptoms and extent of neuronal involvement.
Lymphovascular invasion is a high-risk feature.
